goal_11.config.title: Building productive cities
goal_11.content.#1.text: In just over six decades, the world has become predominantly urban. In 1960, just one billion people lived in cities. Today, that number has surged to 4.7 billion—about 58 percent of the world’s population.
goal_11.content.#2.title: Urban and rural population by region (millions)
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#0.text: But this transformation has not unfolded evenly across regions.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#1.text: Over the past 25 years, [emphasis: Europe] and [emphasis: Central Asia] have seen little overall population growth, with urban and rural populations remaining largely stable.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#2.text: In [emphasis: North America] and[emphasis:  Latin America & the Caribbean], rural populations have plateaued while cities have continued to expand steadily.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#3.text: In [emphasis: East Asia & the Pacific], the shift has been more dramatic: urban populations have grown rapidly as rural populations declined, with urban population overtaking rural in 2008.
goal_11.content.#2.scenes.#4.text: Meanwhile, in [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa], [emphasis: the Middle East and North Africa, Afghanistan] [emphasis: and Pakistan], and [emphasis: South Asia], both rural and urban populations have grown. And in Sub-Saharan Africa and in the Middle East and North Africa region, urban populations have expanded at a much faster pace than rural ones.
goal_11.content.#3.text: Urban expansion is far from over. By 2050, South Asia is projected to be the only region where most people will still live in rural areas.
goal_11.content.#4.text: But the future of urban growth will look different from the past. The world’s largest cities are not expected to grow dramatically larger. Megacities—those with more than 10 million people—increased from 20 in 2000 to 33 in 2025. But over the next 25 years, that number is projected to rise only modestly, to 37.
goal_11.content.#5.text: Instead, urban growth will be driven by smaller cities. Over the past 25 years, the number of small and very small cities—those with fewer than 1 million residents—has expanded from about 8,700 to 11,600. By 2050, their number is projected to approach 15,000.
goal_11.content.#6.text: The next chapter of urbanization will not be defined by ever-larger megacities, but by the rapid spread of smaller urban centers across the globe.
goal_11.content.#7.title: The world cities
goal_11.content.#7.subtitle: Vis subtitle
goal_11.content.#8.text: Urban expansion has happened through two channels: population growth and rural-urban migration. While it is difficult to distinguish the extent of each channel at a global scale, there is some evidence that historically an increase in urban population happened because of rural-urban migration, but in the future, the increase in urban population will largely come from population growth.
goal_11.content.#10.text: Shift away from rural to urban
goal_11.content.#11.text: As agriculture becomes more productive and requires fewer workers, cities become the natural destination for those seeking better opportunities. As workers move from lower-productivity rural jobs into higher-productivity urban industries, overall economic output rises.
goal_11.content.#13.text: Far from being just a demographic shift, urban expansion can be an economic opportunity. Across history, countries have grown richer as they have grown more urban. Why? Because cities create the conditions that make productivity and innovation thrive.
goal_11.content.#14.text: That is why urban expansion often happens along with economic growth.
goal_11.content.#15.title: Urbanization is associated with economic growth
goal_11.content.#15.subtitle: Vis subtitle
goal_11.content.#16.text: Urbanization enables productivity because it benefits both industries and people. As populations concentrate in cities, the forces that drive productivity—scale, specialization, and innovation—tend to strengthen.
goal_11.content.#17.text: When firms cluster together, they can function more efficiently. Being close to suppliers, customers, and competitors lowers costs. Large, dense markets make it easier to find specialized workers and match their functions with skills. Proximity to other firms also fosters knowledge spillovers: ideas spread faster, which boosts innovation and growth. Urban hubs are key nodes in trade and transport networks, reducing shipping and coordination costs for goods and services. Better connectivity makes it easier for firms in cities to access global markets and value chains, which can further accelerate growth.
goal_11.content.#18.text: People benefit too. Cities offer a chance at economic mobility, more diversity in occupations, better pay, and better infrastructure.
goal_11.content.#20.text: Urban areas widen the range of jobs people can do. By concentrating commerce, industry, transport, and services in one place, cities create far more varied labor demand than rural areas, where work is largely centered on agriculture. Cities can support both everyday occupations and highly specialized roles that rural economies cannot sustain. Larger cities, in particular, don’t just offer more of the same jobs—they reorganize work into more specialized and interconnected roles.
goal_11.content.#21.text: Let’s compare the “specialization of each occupation” in urban and rural areas. The specialization indicator shown in the chart below compares the share of each occupation located in urban areas with the share of all jobs located in urban areas. Each dot represents a country. If a particular occupation is more urban-concentrated than employment overall, the indicator is above one for that country. When the value is closer to one, the jobs in this occupation are distributed much like other jobs. Values below one suggest that jobs in this occupation are relatively less concentrated in cities.
goal_11.content.#22.title: Urban areas offer a variety of occupations
goal_11.content.#22.subtitle: Specialized skill indicator
goal_11.content.#22.visdescription: Across almost all occupations—except agriculture, forestry, and fishing—jobs are more concentrated in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#22.textParagraph: Across almost all occupations—except agriculture, forestry, and fishing—jobs are more concentrated in urban areas. This highlights how cities offer a far wider and more diverse set of work opportunities for workers than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#22.label_OCU_ISCO08_8: Plant and machine operators, and assemblers
goal_11.content.#23.text: For many workers, urban labor markets also mean mobility. People rarely stay in one job for long; instead, they move between employers and sectors, and sometimes between informal self-employment and wage work. The density of opportunities in cities makes it easier to keep searching for a better match between skills, preferences, and pay.
goal_11.content.#24.text: This diversity of occupations also acts as a form of insurance. When one industry slows, workers in cities can often shift into other sectors without having to move away. This makes urban economies more resilient and individual careers more adaptable. With more job options close by, people can switch roles, build new skills, and move up the income ladder without uprooting their lives.
goal_11.content.#26.text: This diversity of occupations also means a wider range of jobs across skill levels. In many developing countries, urban labor markets are often described as segmented into two broad tiers: a higher-wage formal sector and a lower-wage informal sector. However, even low-wage informal jobs can offer pathways to build skills, gain experience, and move into better opportunities over time—especially in dense urban markets where transitions between jobs are easier.
goal_11.content.#27.text: Low- and medium-skilled jobs remain more concentrated in rural areas. In urban areas, however, their specialization levels hover around one, meaning these jobs are represented roughly in proportion to overall employment. By contrast, high-skill jobs are strongly concentrated in urban areas, with specialization rates far higher in urban areas than in rural regions—highlighting cities’ role as hubs for advanced and specialized work.
goal_11.content.#28.title: Urban areas offer jobs of a variety of skills
goal_11.content.#28.subtitle: Ratio of employment (urban/urban+rural) by skill level of occupation
goal_11.content.#29.text: Prosperity depends on how well countries attract productive workers, match them to the right jobs, and help them continue to build skills. Cities make this easier because skilled workers learn faster when they are surrounded by peers—people with similar or complementary skills, ideas, and experience. The result is higher productivity in urban areas, reinforcing cities as engines of economic growth.
goal_11.content.#31.text: Jobs in cities tend to be higher skilled, formal, and more productive—and that usually means higher pay. Cities bring together larger and more diverse labor markets, with more firms competing for workers, especially in higher-skill sectors. This competition, along with better infrastructure and job matching, helps push wages up.
goal_11.content.#32.text: Of the 79 countries with data, average hourly wages are higher in urban areas than in rural areas in 66 countries (that is over 80 percent). For example, in Benin and Peru the average hourly wages in urban areas are 40 percent higher than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#33.title: Hourly earning of urban and rural population
goal_11.content.#34.text: Of course, city life is also more expensive. Rents are higher, and congestion and crowding come with real costs. But for many workers and firms, the benefits still outweigh the downsides. Higher pay, better job options, and more opportunities to learn and grow make urban areas attractive. The sheer variety cities offer—of people, services, and experiences—adds value too, helping compensate for the higher cost of living.
goal_11.content.#36.text: Cities are generally better equipped with infrastructure services like clean drinking water, safely managed sanitation, transport, electricity, healthcare, education etc. It forms the basis for a productive and good quality of life.
goal_11.content.#38.text: Expanding education systems in urban areas is easier and costs less than expanding it in rural areas. Returns to education are thus generally found to be higher in urban than rural areas. The effect of urbanization on education is generally positive, and empirical literature shows this correlation especially in Asia. Urban populations have more chance to reach hospitals, care centers and sanitation. Health care systems are also more developed, which may lead to better health performance than those in rural areas. Moreover, urban workers have better access to transport and to other facilities such as water, the Internet and electricity. Firms and workers may have higher productivity in urban than rural areas.
goal_11.content.#40.text: Cities have the potential to boost productivity and incomes—but urbanization doesn’t automatically lead to development.
goal_11.content.#41.text: Mixed-use, walkable, economically integrated, and transitrich places define good urbanism in any city, anywhere on the planet.
goal_11.content.#42.text: However, urban expansion can be poorly managed leading to overcrowding and urban sprawl.
goal_11.content.#44.text: Rapid population growth without sufficient urban planning can lead to overcrowding, placing pressure on infrastructure, increasing congestion, and straining basic services. Formal housing markets tend to serve middle- and higher-income households, leaving poorer residents—especially migrants arriving from rural areas—with few options but to settle informally. The growth of slums reflects this imbalance: demand for housing rises faster than governments and markets can provide affordable homes.
goal_11.content.#45.text: Today, about [emphasis: 23% of the world’s urban population—roughly 1.8 billion people—live in slum conditions], with the challenge particularly acute in low-income countries where progress in reducing slum populations has slowed. Although the share of people living in slums has declined globally over the past decades, progress often stalls after an initial improvement. In [emphasis: Latin America and the Caribbean], for example, the slum population share fell steadily in the early 2000s but has hovered around [emphasis: 17–19% in recent years]. Similarly, in Sub-Saharan Africa, slum population fell from every 6 out of 10 urban residents living slums to about 5 out of 10 in 2020. But since 2020, the share of people living in slums is rising again.
goal_11.content.#46.text: Economic growth can help reduce slums by raising incomes and enabling greater investment in housing and infrastructure, but without careful urban planning and adequate housing supply, slums can persist even in growing economies.
goal_11.content.#48.text: [emphasis: Urban sprawl] is a pattern of [emphasis: low-density, outward expansion of cities], where development spreads across large areas with homes, jobs, and services far apart, making cities physically larger without necessarily becoming more populated or productive. Typical characteristics of urban sprawl include low-density housing, separation of residential, commercial, and work areas, heavy reliance on cars, and rapid expansion of urban land relative to population.
goal_11.content.#49.text: In wealthy countries it often takes the form of low-density suburban expansion dominated by cars, while in many developing countries [emphasis: low-income sprawl] pushes poorer households to the metropolitan edge, isolating them from jobs, services, and infrastructure. In parts of Asia, [emphasis: high-density sprawl] appears as large superblocks of single-use high-rises that weaken walkability and urban connections. Despite their differences, all forms of sprawl fragment cities, lengthen commutes, increase congestion and pollution, and undermine the vibrant, connected urban environments that support economic opportunity.
goal_11.content.#50.text: Sprawl raises the cost of building and maintaining infrastructure because roads, utilities, and services must cover larger areas. It can also increase inequality by placing housing, especially for poorer people, far from jobs and services. Low-density development encourages car dependence, leading to congestion, pollution, and higher transport costs. And finally, sprawl increases land consumption, carbon emissions, and ecosystem destruction. Overall, sprawling cities tend to be less efficient, less inclusive, and more environmentally damaging.
goal_11.content.#51.text: weak infrastructure, and fragile institutions—cities can end up with high living costs, slums, and social exclusion. Instead of amplifying opportunity, these pressures can hold productivity back. Therefore, it is important to plan cities well.
goal_11.content.#52.title: Urban development in Querétaro, Mexico
goal_11.content.#53.title: Urban development in Adelaide, Australia
goal_11.content.#54.text: Building productive cities for the future
goal_11.content.#55.text: As cities expand, much more land will need to be planned and serviced. Built-up areas are projected to grow by more than [emphasis: 50 percent over the next 25 years], largely in middle-income countries, making the way cities expand increasingly important. Ensuring that new urban areas remain connected and well served will be critical for maintaining productivity. Productive cities depend on integrated labor markets where people can easily reach jobs. This requires promoting spatially connected development, encouraging sufficient density without overcrowding, and strengthening transport systems so workers can access opportunities within reasonable commute times. Careful planning today will determine whether expanding cities support opportunity and productivity in the future.
